name: Wai-O-Tapu
description: Wai-O-Tapu sits south of Rotorua and is one of the most colourful geothermal landscapes anywhere on Earth. Boiling mud pools, citrus-bright crater lakes and the Lady Knox Geyser sit inside a walking-loop volcanic park. The Māori name means 'sacred waters' — the area was used by local iwi for centuries before tourism arrived.
things_to_do: Walk the three signposted loops (30, 45, 75 minutes)
Catch the 10:15am Lady Knox Geyser eruption
Photograph the Champagne Pool — the colours are not edited
Visit the nearby Mud Pool just before sunset
best_season: Year-round; mornings are quieter and the steam is more dramatic in cooler months.
transit_context: Roughly 30 minutes south of Rotorua by car on SH5. No public transport — rental car or organised tour from Rotorua.

food.name: Eat Streat Rotorua
food.note: Restaurant strip in town with covered awning and underground geothermal heating.

food.name: Te Whekau Cafe
food.note: Casual lakeside cafe on the way back into Rotorua.

accommodation.name: Rotorua town centre hotels
accommodation.note: Best base — 30 minutes drive from Wai-O-Tapu, full range of options.

accommodation.name: Waikite Valley Thermal Pools campground
accommodation.note: Camp site with private hot pools, 10 minutes from Wai-O-Tapu.

walks.name: Rainbow Mountain Track
walks.note: 1–3 hour climb just up the road, summit views over the geothermal area.

walks.name: Kerosene Creek
walks.note: Free natural hot stream 10 minutes drive away — popular with locals.

name: Tongariro Alpine Crossing
description: Often called the best one-day walk in the world, the Tongariro Alpine Crossing traverses 19.4 kilometres of active volcanic terrain inside a dual UNESCO World Heritage area. You'll cross old lava flows, skirt the Emerald Lakes, and look down on the Red Crater. Tongariro is a tapu mountain — the walk gives the summit cone a wide and respectful berth.
things_to_do: Start before 8am to beat the afternoon clouds
Photograph the Emerald Lakes from the Red Crater rim
Use the shuttle service — it's a one-way walk
Pack alpine layers even in summer; weather changes fast
best_season: November to April. The crossing is an alpine route in winter and requires guides plus crampons.
transit_context: Shuttles run from National Park Village, Whakapapa, and Taupō. The walk is one-way so a shuttle is essentially mandatory.

food.name: Station Cafe, National Park
food.note: Classic before-and-after walker stop. Big breakfasts.

food.name: Schnapps Bar, National Park
food.note: Pub food and pints, good for a post-walk beer.

accommodation.name: Skotel Alpine Resort, Whakapapa
accommodation.note: Closest hotel to the start; book months ahead in summer.

accommodation.name: National Park Village motels
accommodation.note: Cluster of small motels and lodges, well-served by shuttles.

walks.name: Taranaki Falls Loop
walks.note: 2-hour walk near Whakapapa Village — easy alternative if the crossing is closed.

walks.name: Tama Lakes Track
walks.note: 5–6 hour return to two crater lakes; quieter than the crossing.

name: Aoraki / Mount Cook
description: Aoraki is the tallest mountain in Aotearoa at 3,724 metres — and a tipuna, an ancestor, to Ngāi Tahu. The bilingual name reflects an official 1998 Treaty of Waitangi settlement. The valley below is a dark sky reserve, and the Hooker Valley track is one of the country's most accessible alpine walks.
things_to_do: Walk the Hooker Valley Track to the glacial lake (3 hours return)
Stay overnight for stargazing — the valley is an international dark sky reserve
Visit the Sir Edmund Hillary Alpine Centre at The Hermitage
Take a scenic flight over the Tasman Glacier if the weather is clear
best_season: December to March for walks; July to September for snow and stargazing.
transit_context: About 4.5 hours by car from Christchurch via Lake Tekapo. Limited bus services — most visitors drive or join a tour.

food.name: Old Mountaineers' Cafe
food.note: Climber's cafe at Mount Cook Village — generous portions, photo-lined walls.

food.name: The Hermitage Panorama Room
food.note: Restaurant with full Aoraki views; book ahead at sunset.

accommodation.name: The Hermitage Hotel
accommodation.note: Iconic lodge at the head of the valley — rooms vary in price and view.

accommodation.name: Aoraki/Mount Cook Alpine Lodge
accommodation.note: Smaller and more affordable, still walking distance to trailheads.

walks.name: Hooker Valley Track
walks.note: The signature walk — three swing bridges and the lake at the end.

walks.name: Sealy Tarns Track
walks.note: Steep stairs up to alpine tarns; the harder cousin to Hooker Valley.

name: Hokitika
description: Hokitika is a small West Coast town with a 19th-century gold-rush past and a 21st-century pounamu (greenstone) trade. The driftwood beach, the glow worm dell after dark, and the gorge with its impossible turquoise water are the three things almost every visitor leaves with photos of.
things_to_do: Visit the Hokitika Gorge — the blue is from glacial rock flour, not filters
Walk Hokitika Beach at sunset and find the famous driftwood sign
Step into the free Glow Worm Dell after dark on Tudor Street
Browse pounamu workshops on Tancred Street — buy from a Ngāi Tahu-licensed carver
best_season: October to April. The West Coast is rainy year-round; pack a jacket regardless.
transit_context: On SH6, about 3 hours south of Greymouth or 7 hours from Christchurch via Arthur's Pass. The TranzAlpine train runs to Greymouth and pairs well with a hire car onward.

food.name: Stumpers Cafe & Bar
food.note: Long-running local pub with hearty meals and West Coast whitebait in season.

food.name: Fat Pipi Pizzas
food.note: Casual wood-fired pizza, often lined out the door at dinner.

accommodation.name: Beachfront Hotel Hokitika
accommodation.note: Sea-facing rooms; sunsets straight off the Tasman.

accommodation.name: Shining Star Beachfront Accommodation
accommodation.note: Cabins and chalets with a small farmyard — good for families.

walks.name: Hokitika Gorge Walkway
walks.note: 30-minute loop on a swing bridge over the impossibly blue river.

walks.name: Lake Kaniere Track
walks.note: Forested lakeside walks 15 minutes inland; quiet midweek.

name: Waiheke Island
description: Waiheke is a 40-minute ferry ride from downtown Auckland and operates on a different clock to the city. Olive groves, vineyards on north-facing slopes, and a string of swimmable bays make it a day trip many visitors regret not staying longer on. The locals call it 'the island' as if there were no others.
things_to_do: Vineyard-hop on the wine bus — Mudbrick, Cable Bay, Stonyridge
Swim at Onetangi Beach (long) or Palm Beach (sheltered)
Walk the Te Ara Hura coastal track — pick a 2-3 hour section
Catch the last ferry back at golden hour for the city skyline view
best_season: November to April for the beaches; vineyards and restaurants are open year-round.
transit_context: Fullers and Sealink ferries from Auckland CBD's Queen's Wharf. 40 minutes passenger, ~1 hour vehicle. Local buses connect the main bays.

food.name: Mudbrick Vineyard
food.note: Hilltop restaurant with city skyline views — the long-lunch classic.

food.name: The Oyster Inn, Oneroa
food.note: Small dining room and oyster bar in the main village; book ahead.

accommodation.name: Oneroa village apartments
accommodation.note: Most central — walk to the beach, restaurants, and ferry bus.

accommodation.name: Delamore Lodge
accommodation.note: Boutique luxury with Hauraki Gulf views; splurge territory.

walks.name: Te Ara Hura — Matiatia to Oneroa
walks.note: 1.5-hour cliff-top section right from the ferry terminal.

walks.name: Stony Batter Historic Reserve
walks.note: World War II tunnels and headland walks at the island's east end.

name: Cape Reinga
description: Cape Reinga sits at the very top of Aotearoa, where the Tasman Sea and Pacific Ocean meet in a visible churn of currents. In Māori belief it is Te Rerenga Wairua — the leaping place of spirits, where the souls of the dead depart for Hawaiki. It deserves to be visited quietly.
things_to_do: Walk down to the lighthouse and watch the two oceans collide
Drive Ninety Mile Beach southbound (only with a 4WD or guided tour)
Visit Te Paki sand dunes and rent a boogie board
Stop at the giant Te Hāpua kauri on the way north
best_season: November to April. The Far North is warm year-round but coastal wind picks up in winter.
transit_context: About 1 hour 45 from Kaitāia by sealed road. Most visitors come from the Bay of Islands as a long day trip or stay in Kaitāia overnight.

food.name: Tales of the Tail, Kaitāia
food.note: Fish and chips done well — the practical pre-Cape lunch stop.

food.name: Mangonui Fish Shop
food.note: Iconic harbourside fish-and-chip shop on the way north from Kerikeri.

accommodation.name: Kaitāia motels
accommodation.note: Most northern town with a full range of motels — 1.5 hours from the cape.

accommodation.name: Waitiki Landing
accommodation.note: The closest stay to the cape; a campground and basic cabins.

walks.name: Cape Reinga Coastal Walk
walks.note: Multi-day option south to Te Paki; or just the 1-hour cape circuit.

walks.name: Te Paki Stream Track
walks.note: Walk through a sandy streambed to the dunes — surreal landscape.

name: Milford Sound / Piopiotahi
description: Piopiotahi was carved by glaciers and is now reached by a road that is itself a sightseeing event. Mitre Peak rises 1,692 metres straight from the water. Rain — and there is a lot of it — turns every cliff face into a temporary waterfall. It rewards visitors who slow down.
things_to_do: Take a 1.5–2 hour boat cruise out to the Tasman Sea
Stop on the Milford Road at The Chasm and Mirror Lakes
Kayak in the early morning before the cruise boats start
Visit the Milford Discovery Centre underwater observatory
best_season: October to April for milder driving; rain photography is best year-round.
transit_context: About 2 hours from Te Anau on the Milford Road (SH94). Buses run from Queenstown and Te Anau. The drive itself is the main event — allow stops.

food.name: Milford Sound Lodge cafe
food.note: The only meaningful food stop close to the sound itself.

food.name: Te Anau township restaurants
food.note: Stock up here before the drive — limited options past Te Anau.

accommodation.name: Milford Sound Lodge
accommodation.note: The only accommodation right at the sound — book months ahead.

accommodation.name: Te Anau motels and lakefront hotels
accommodation.note: Most visitors stay in Te Anau and drive in for the day.

walks.name: Key Summit Track
walks.note: 3-hour return on the Milford Road — alpine views without the crowds.

walks.name: Lake Marian Track
walks.note: Full-day walk to a hanging valley lake — well-marked but steep.

name: Tāmaki Makaurau (Auckland)
description: Tāmaki Makaurau means 'Tāmaki desired by many' — and its 53 volcanic cones, two harbours, and warm-climate beaches explain why. Auckland is a Polynesian city as much as it is a colonial one, with the largest Polynesian population of any city in the world. It rewards visitors who get out of the CBD.
things_to_do: Walk up Maungawhau (Mount Eden) at sunrise for a 360° city view
Eat your way through K' Road and the Karangahape food laneways
Take the ferry to Devonport for a half-day from the CBD
Visit Auckland War Memorial Museum on the Domain — the Pacific gallery in particular
best_season: Year-round. Summer (December–March) for beaches; the city itself is comfortable in any season.
transit_context: Auckland International Airport is the main gateway to Aotearoa. The train and inner-city link buses cover most of the central city.

food.name: Cassia, CBD
food.note: Modern Indian; one of Auckland's most awarded restaurants.

food.name: Hawker Hawker, Karangahape Road
food.note: Casual Southeast Asian street food, late-night friendly.

accommodation.name: QT Auckland Viaduct
accommodation.note: Design-forward hotel by the harbour — walking distance to the ferry terminal.

accommodation.name: Hotel DeBrett, CBD
accommodation.note: Boutique hotel in a restored heritage building, central but quiet.

walks.name: Coast to Coast Walkway
walks.note: 16km city walk from Waitematā to Manukau Harbour, mostly through parks and volcanic cones.

walks.name: Rangitoto Island Summit Track
walks.note: Day trip — ferry plus 1-hour climb to a 600-year-old volcanic summit.

name: Waikato
description: The Waikato is a wide, fertile region centred on Aotearoa's longest river. Hamilton — Kirikiriroa in Te Reo — sits in the middle of dairy country and is the launchpad for two of the country's most-photographed sights: Hobbiton in Matamata and the Waitomo Glow-worm Caves. Slow-paced compared to its neighbours, by design.
things_to_do: Tour Hobbiton Movie Set in Matamata — the actual Lord of the Rings shire
Boat through the Waitomo Glow-worm Caves at dusk
Wander Hamilton Gardens — themed gardens running from Italian Renaissance to Māori
Cycle a section of Te Awa River Ride along the Waikato
best_season: Year-round; spring (Sep–Nov) for green pastures.
transit_context: Roughly 1.5 hours south of Auckland on SH1. Hamilton has frequent buses from Auckland and Wellington.

food.name: Riverside Cafe, Hamilton
food.note: Long-running brunch spot overlooking the Waikato River.

food.name: Mr Pickles, Hamilton
food.note: Casual modern pub food in the Victoria Street strip.

accommodation.name: Hamilton CBD hotels
accommodation.note: Best base — full range of options, walking distance to the river.

accommodation.name: Hobbiton Lodge, Matamata
accommodation.note: On-site at the Hobbiton set; book months ahead in summer.

walks.name: Te Aroha summit
walks.note: 3-hour return climb up Mt Te Aroha; views over the Hauraki Plains.

walks.name: Hakarimata Walkway
walks.note: Steep but short forest climb near Ngāruawāhia, with kauri.

name: Taupō
description: Taupō sits on the shore of Aotearoa's largest lake — a caldera left by a supervolcanic eruption around 1,800 years ago. Crystal-clear water, world-class trout fishing, geothermal hot springs along the river, and the natural staging post for a Tongariro climb.
things_to_do: Cruise to the Mine Bay Māori rock carvings — only reachable by boat
Walk to Huka Falls — 220,000 litres a second through a 15m gorge
Soak at Spa Park's free natural hot stream beside the Waikato River
Try a tandem skydive — Taupō is one of the cheapest skydive spots in the country
best_season: Year-round; summer for swimming, winter for crisp air and clearer skies.
transit_context: Roughly 3 hours from Auckland on SH1, 4 hours from Wellington. Small regional airport with flights from Auckland.

food.name: Pauly's Diner, Taupō
food.note: Lakefront breakfast/brunch institution.

food.name: Plateau Restaurant, Taupō
food.note: Modern NZ in town; good wine list, book ahead at weekends.

accommodation.name: Hilton Lake Taupō
accommodation.note: Heritage building on the lakeshore — best for a splurge.

accommodation.name: Taupō lakefront motels
accommodation.note: Plenty along Lake Terrace with pools and easy walking access to town.

walks.name: Huka Falls Walkway
walks.note: 1-hour return along the Waikato River from town to the falls.

walks.name: Mt Tauhara summit
walks.note: 3-hour return climb on the eastern side of town; full lake panorama.

name: Tauranga
description: Tauranga and its iconic Mount Maunganui — Mauao — define the Bay of Plenty. Wide white-sand beaches, a sunny microclimate, surf breaks rolling in off the Pacific, and the country's largest port hidden behind a green volcanic plug. The Mount itself is the photo every Kiwi has on their camera roll.
things_to_do: Climb Mauao at sunrise — 45 minutes up, the whole bay laid out below
Surf or swim at Mount Main Beach — patrolled, 3km of sand
Walk the Mauao base track — 45 minutes around the headland at sea level
Cycle the harbour-side path from Tauranga to Mt Maunganui across the bridge
best_season: November to April for beach time; the Mount climb is year-round.
transit_context: About 2.5 hours from Auckland by car. Tauranga Airport has direct flights from Auckland, Wellington, and Christchurch.

food.name: Macau, Tauranga
food.note: Modern Asian fusion in the harbourside Strand precinct.

food.name: Rising Tide, Mt Maunganui
food.note: Beachfront breakfast spot; queues out the door on weekends.

accommodation.name: Trinity Wharf Hotel, Tauranga
accommodation.note: Modern hotel right on the harbour with a pool.

accommodation.name: Mount Maunganui beachfront motels
accommodation.note: Several within a 5-minute walk of Main Beach — book early in summer.

walks.name: Mauao base track
walks.note: Easy 45-minute coastal loop around the headland.

walks.name: Papamoa Hills Cultural Heritage Park
walks.note: 1.5-hour ridgeline walk through ancient pā sites.

name: Rotorua
description: Rotorua is the country's geothermal heart and its most accessible introduction to Māori culture. Steam drifts up from streetside drains, the lake's edge bubbles, and the air carries the unmistakable sulphur smell. Te Arawa iwi have lived alongside the geothermal forces for centuries — their cultural performances and ngāwhā (hot pools) are the city's defining experience.
things_to_do: Visit Te Puia or Whakarewarewa Living Village — Māori cultural centres around active geysers
Soak at Polynesian Spa on the lake's edge — separate adult and family pools
Mountain bike the Whakarewarewa Forest (Redwoods) — world-class trails through giant Californian redwoods
Take the Skyline gondola up Mount Ngongotahā for the luge and the view
best_season: Year-round; cooler months make the geothermal steam more dramatic.
transit_context: About 3 hours from Auckland on SH1/SH5. Rotorua Airport has direct flights from Auckland, Wellington, and Christchurch.

food.name: Eat Streat
food.note: Covered restaurant strip in the centre of town with underground geothermal heating.

food.name: Atticus Finch
food.note: Modern Mediterranean small plates, generous portions, in the centre.

accommodation.name: Pullman Rotorua
accommodation.note: Best central full-service hotel; lake views from upper floors.

accommodation.name: Lakefront motels
accommodation.note: Cluster along Lake Road — most have private hot pools fed from the geothermal field.

walks.name: Lake Rotorua trail
walks.note: Easy 26km loop around the lake — do a section, or rent a bike.

walks.name: Redwoods Treewalk
walks.note: Suspended walkway through the redwood canopy — magical at dusk with lights.

name: Whangārei
description: Northland's only city. Sub-tropical, harbour-fronted, and the staging post for everything north of Auckland. The Town Basin marina is a small-but-good food and art precinct, and the Hundertwasser Art Centre — opened in 2022 — is the region's most distinctive piece of architecture.
things_to_do: Walk to Whangārei Falls — short loop through native bush to a 26m drop
Visit the Hundertwasser Art Centre and Wairau Māori Art Gallery in the Town Basin
Climb Mount Manaia — steep 90-minute return for harbour views
Drive 30 minutes to Tutukaka for diving the Poor Knights Islands
best_season: Year-round; sub-tropical even in winter, summer (Dec–Feb) for swimming and diving.
transit_context: About 2 hours north of Auckland on SH1. Whangārei has a small regional airport.

food.name: Killer Prawn
food.note: Long-running waterfront restaurant; the prawn dish it's named after is the order.

food.name: Vinyl Café, Town Basin
food.note: Casual brunch by the marina, good coffee.

accommodation.name: Distinction Hotel Whangārei
accommodation.note: Central, walkable to the Town Basin.

accommodation.name: Town Basin apartments
accommodation.note: Self-contained units overlooking the marina.

walks.name: Whangārei Falls Loop
walks.note: 20-minute easy loop through bush to the falls.

walks.name: Mount Manaia summit
walks.note: 1.5-hour return; steep but short, with chains on the upper section.

name: Whakatāne
description: Whakatāne is officially the sunniest town in the country and one of the most Māori — Mātaatua iwi sit at the heart of it. The river mouth, the cliffs, and the long curve of Ohope Beach (often voted Aotearoa's best) are the draw. Whakaari/White Island sits offshore, though tours haven't run since the 2019 eruption.
things_to_do: Walk Ohope Beach — 11km of sand, 10 minutes drive from town
Climb the Kohi Point Walkway from town to Ohope along the clifftops
Visit Te Whare Taonga o Taketake — the Whakatāne Museum, strong on local Māori history
Kayak or jet boat up the Whakatāne River into the gorge
best_season: Spring through autumn; summer for the beach.
transit_context: About 1 hour east of Rotorua, 90 minutes from Tauranga. Small regional airport with flights from Auckland.

food.name: The Stage Door Cafe
food.note: Long-running brunch institution off The Strand.

food.name: Roquette Restaurant
food.note: Modern NZ on The Strand; reliably good for dinner.

accommodation.name: Beachpoint Resort, Ohope
accommodation.note: Apartments fronting Ohope Beach — best base if you want sand and sun.

accommodation.name: Whakatāne Hotel
accommodation.note: Heritage hotel in the centre of town, walkable to everything.

walks.name: Otarawairere Beach loop
walks.note: 1.5-hour clifftop loop from Ohope — hidden beach reveal halfway.

walks.name: Kohi Point Walkway
walks.note: 3-hour clifftop walk from town to Ohope, or section of it.

name: Whanganui
description: Whanganui is a Victorian river town with a slow, distinctive heart. The Whanganui River — Te Awa Tupua — was the first river anywhere in the world to be granted legal personhood, recognised in 2017 as a living being with the same rights as a person. The town reflects that relationship: small galleries, riverside walks, and a heritage main street.
things_to_do: Ride the Durie Hill Elevator — a 1919 underground tunnel and lift up to a memorial tower
Visit the Sarjeant Gallery — one of NZ's strongest regional art collections
Walk or cycle the riverside path from the town through to Aramoho
Paddle a section of the Whanganui Journey — multi-day canoe trip on the river
best_season: Year-round; the river is most paddleable Oct–April.
transit_context: About 2.5 hours by car from Wellington on SH3. Small airport with flights from Auckland.

food.name: Caroline's Boatshed
food.note: Restaurant on the river bank — book ahead, especially in summer.

food.name: Mint Café
food.note: Reliable brunch spot in the heritage main street.

accommodation.name: Rutland Arms Inn
accommodation.note: Heritage hotel above an English-style pub in the centre.

accommodation.name: Aotea Motor Lodge
accommodation.note: Comfortable modern motel within walking distance of town.

walks.name: Bushy Park Sanctuary
walks.note: Predator-fenced reserve 30 minutes drive away — kiwi and saddleback.

walks.name: Bason Botanic Gardens
walks.note: Free gardens 15 minutes drive west of town — good loop walks.

name: Taranaki
description: Mount Taranaki is a near-perfect cone — the kind of mountain children draw. Standing 2,518m above the Taranaki coast, it dominates everything. The surrounding Egmont National Park has alpine walks for every fitness level, the city of New Plymouth has one of NZ's best contemporary art galleries, and the Surf Highway runs the dark-sand coast.
things_to_do: Walk the Pouakai Crossing — 19km full-day alpine traverse around the mountain
Visit the Govett-Brewster Art Gallery / Len Lye Centre in New Plymouth
Walk the New Plymouth Coastal Walkway — 13km flat seafront path
Loop the Wilkies Pools track — 1-hour family-friendly forest walk
best_season: November to April for alpine walks. The mountain has weather year-round; pack layers.
transit_context: About 5 hours by car from Auckland or 4 from Wellington. New Plymouth has direct flights from Auckland, Wellington, and Christchurch.

food.name: Snug Lounge, New Plymouth
food.note: Late-night cocktail and small-plates bar — quiet but excellent.

food.name: Federal Store
food.note: Casual all-day cafe with consistent breakfast.

accommodation.name: Plymouth International Hotel
accommodation.note: Most central full-service hotel in New Plymouth.

accommodation.name: Dawson Falls Mountain Lodge
accommodation.note: Inside the National Park — the closest stay to the alpine walks.

walks.name: Pouakai Crossing
walks.note: The signature Taranaki day walk — full day, alpine, photogenic.

walks.name: Wilkies Pools Loop
walks.note: Easy hour-long loop in the rainforest near Dawson Falls.

name: Ōtautahi (Christchurch)
description: Christchurch is rebuilding — a decade and a half after the 2011 earthquake, the result is a thoughtful, deliberately replanted Garden City. Modern timber architecture sits alongside heritage stone; the Avon River winds through the centre; the Banks Peninsula and the Southern Alps are both an hour away. The South Island's main international gateway.
things_to_do: Walk through the Botanic Gardens — 21 hectares in the heart of town
Visit the Cardboard Cathedral — temporary church that became permanent
Eat your way through Riverside Market — best single food destination in the city
Punt on the Avon, then take a tram around the rebuilt centre
Day trip to Akaroa on Banks Peninsula — French-influenced harbour village
best_season: Spring (Sep–Nov) for the gardens, autumn (Mar–May) for clear skies and colour.
transit_context: Christchurch International Airport is the main South Island gateway. Walking-friendly central city, with trams and free city buses.

food.name: Riverside Market
food.note: Multi-stall food hall in a beautiful heritage shell.

food.name: Twenty Seven Steps
food.note: Modern NZ above New Regent Street — book ahead.

accommodation.name: The George Christchurch
accommodation.note: Boutique hotel facing Hagley Park — long the city's best.

accommodation.name: Sudima City Christchurch
accommodation.note: Modern central hotel; reliable mid-range option.

walks.name: Bridle Path, Lyttelton
walks.note: 1.5-hour climb from Heathcote Valley to Lyttelton over the Port Hills.

walks.name: Port Hills tracks
walks.note: Network of trails behind the city; pick a section, panoramic views.

name: Wellington
description: Aotearoa's compact, opinionated capital. Wellington — Te Whanganui-a-Tara, 'the great harbour of Tara' — sits in a near-perfect amphitheatre of green hills around an enormous harbour. Famous for coffee culture, the world-class Te Papa Tongarewa museum, government and parliament, and the wind. Easily the most walkable major city in the country.
things_to_do: Spend half a day at Te Papa Tongarewa — six floors, free, world-class
Eat your way through Cuba Street and the Hannahs Laneway food precinct
Ride the Wellington Cable Car to the Botanic Gardens, walk back down through it
Visit Zealandia — predator-fenced sanctuary 10 minutes from town with kākā, takahē, tūī
Catch the Bluebridge or Interislander ferry across Cook Strait to the South Island
best_season: November to April. The wind is famous; spring and autumn can be the most settled.
transit_context: Wellington International Airport with direct flights from across NZ and Australia. Train and bus links north. 3-hour ferry to Picton for the South Island.

food.name: Logan Brown
food.note: Long-running modern NZ in a heritage banking hall — the special-occasion pick.

food.name: Fidel's Cafe, Cuba Street
food.note: Cuba Street brunch institution, open since the early '90s.

accommodation.name: QT Wellington
accommodation.note: Boutique hotel on the harbourside; design-forward and central.

accommodation.name: Bolton Hotel
accommodation.note: Reliable mid-range with full kitchens, walking distance to parliament.

walks.name: Mt Victoria Lookout
walks.note: 1-hour return from the city, full panorama of harbour and city.

walks.name: City to Sea Walkway
walks.note: 12km from the harbour to Island Bay along Wellington's spine.

name: Queenstown
description: Aotearoa's adventure capital, on the shore of Lake Whakatipu under the jagged Remarkables range. Bungy jumping was invented commercially here in 1988, and the town remains the gateway for skiing in winter, the Routeburn and Milford day trips, and the surrounding Central Otago wine country.
things_to_do: Cruise Lake Whakatipu on the TSS Earnslaw — a coal-fired vintage steamship still in daily service
Take the Skyline gondola up Bob's Peak for the view, the luge, and dinner
Ski The Remarkables or Coronet Peak (Jun–Sep) — both within 30 minutes of town
Hike Ben Lomond — full-day return, 1,748m summit straight above town
Wine-taste in Gibbston Valley — the world's southernmost commercial wine region
best_season: June to September for ski; November to April for hiking, wine, and lake time.
transit_context: Queenstown Airport with direct flights from major NZ and Australian cities. About 4 hours by car from Christchurch via the spectacular Lindis Pass.

food.name: Fergburger
food.note: The iconic queue is real — go off-peak. Burgers do live up to the hype.

food.name: Botswana Butchery
food.note: Lakefront fine dining; book ahead for a window table.

accommodation.name: Eichardt's Private Hotel
accommodation.note: Heritage boutique hotel right on the lakefront; high-end.

accommodation.name: QT Queenstown
accommodation.note: Design-forward hotel on the lake just north of town.

walks.name: Tiki Trail
walks.note: Steep 45-minute walk up to the Skyline gondola top — alternative to riding up.

walks.name: Ben Lomond Track
walks.note: Full-day, 1,438m climb to a true alpine summit. Serious — pack layers.

name: Dunedin
description: Dunedin is Aotearoa's heritage city — Edwardian and Victorian architecture survives at a scale you don't see anywhere else in the country. The Otago Peninsula, fifteen minutes from town, holds yellow-eyed penguins, royal albatross, and fur seals, all viewable from public reserves. The University of Otago is the country's oldest, and the student culture gives the place its energy.
things_to_do: Tour the Royal Albatross Centre and yellow-eyed penguin reserves on the Otago Peninsula
Walk Baldwin Street — by some measures the steepest residential street in the world (35% gradient)
Explore the heritage stone of the railway station, university quad, and First Church
Visit Larnach Castle — Aotearoa's only castle, on the Peninsula
Head to Tunnel Beach via the cliff-cut tunnel — best at low tide
best_season: Year-round; September to April for albatross and penguin viewing, autumn for clear skies.
transit_context: Dunedin Airport with flights from Auckland, Wellington, Christchurch. About 4.5 hours by car south of Christchurch on SH1.

food.name: Plato Cafe
food.note: Long-running waterfront restaurant; quirky decor, consistent kitchen.

food.name: No 7 Balmac
food.note: Modern bistro in a heritage suburb; Otago wines done well.

accommodation.name: Larnach Lodge
accommodation.note: Stay on the Larnach Castle grounds — limited rooms, book months ahead.

accommodation.name: Distinction Dunedin
accommodation.note: Central, walkable to the Octagon and the railway station.

walks.name: Tunnel Beach Walkway
walks.note: 45 minutes return through farmland to a tunnel-accessed cove.

walks.name: Otago Peninsula coastal walks
walks.note: Pick a section — Sandfly Bay, Allan's Beach, all 30-60 min.

name: Napier
description: Napier was levelled by a 1931 earthquake and rebuilt almost entirely in Art Deco — the largest concentration of 1930s architecture anywhere in the southern hemisphere. The surrounding Hawke's Bay is one of Aotearoa's two great wine regions (alongside Marlborough), and the Cape Kidnappers gannet colony just south is the largest mainland gannetry in the world.
things_to_do: Walk the self-guided Art Deco trail — pick up a map at the Art Deco Centre
Wine-taste at Mission Estate (NZ's oldest winery), Craggy Range, or Te Mata
Visit Cape Kidnappers gannet colony — long beach walk or vehicle tour
Climb Te Mata Peak for the Hawke's Bay panorama
Time a visit for the Art Deco Festival in February — vintage cars, cocktail attire, the works
best_season: February to April for harvest events and Art Deco Festival; year-round for the architecture.
transit_context: Hawke's Bay Airport, just north of Napier. About 4.5 hours by car from Wellington, 5 from Auckland.

food.name: Bistronomy
food.note: Napier's modern NZ flagship — local produce, Hawke's Bay wines.

food.name: Mister D
food.note: Long-running local institution; the doughnuts-and-syringes dessert is the photo.

accommodation.name: Masonic Hotel
accommodation.note: Original Art Deco hotel on Marine Parade; rooms vary, ask about the heritage ones.

accommodation.name: The Crown Hotel, Ahuriri
accommodation.note: Boutique waterfront in the historic port precinct just north of town.

walks.name: Te Mata Peak
walks.note: 30-minute hike up from the carpark, or a longer loop. Big Hawke's Bay views.

walks.name: Marine Parade walk
walks.note: Easy seafront promenade past the Sound Shell and the gardens.

name: Nelson
description: Nelson — Whakatū — sits at the top of the South Island and reliably tops the country's annual sunshine charts (around 2,500 hours a year). The geographic centre of New Zealand is on a small hill above the town. Nelson is the gateway to Abel Tasman National Park and has a strong art-and-craft scene of its own; the Saturday Market is the country's largest.
things_to_do: Spend a day in Abel Tasman National Park — kayak, water-taxi, or walk a section of the Coast Track
Browse the Nelson Saturday Market — the largest in the country, year-round
Visit the World of Wearable Art (WOW) Museum — the headline garments from the annual show
Walk to the Centre of New Zealand monument on Botanical Hill above town
Day trip to Nelson Lakes National Park — Lake Rotoiti, jetty photo, and walks
best_season: January to April for the Abel Tasman beaches and clear skies; year-round otherwise.
transit_context: Nelson Airport with flights from Auckland, Wellington, Christchurch. About 2 hours by car from the Picton ferry terminal on SH6.

food.name: Hopgood's & Co
food.note: Long-running modern NZ in the centre — the dining room locals book first.

food.name: Indian Cafe
food.note: Casual Indian institution; reliable lunch.

accommodation.name: Rutherford Hotel Nelson
accommodation.note: Largest central hotel; central location, walking distance to everything.

accommodation.name: The Wakefield Quay House
accommodation.note: Boutique B&B on the waterfront — book months ahead.

walks.name: Centre of New Zealand
walks.note: 30-minute return uphill walk from the Botanical Reserve to the marker.

walks.name: Abel Tasman Coastal Track
walks.note: Multi-day Great Walk, but day-walk-friendly with water-taxi pickup.
